[OpenAI Agents Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/agents)

questions.stem:
A technology team has built an AI agent that drafts and sends customer emails. During testing, they discover that occasionally the agent sends emails with incorrect product pricing. They do not want to add a human-in-the-loop step because it slows the workflow.

What is the MOST appropriate technical mitigation?

options.text: Accept the pricing errors as an acceptable rate of failure for agent workflows
Add an automated validation step that checks prices in the draft against a live pricing database before sending, and route flagged emails to a human queue
Rewrite the agent's prompt to instruct it not to make pricing mistakes
Send the emails only on weekdays when the team can monitor more easily

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because an automated validation step — programmatically checking prices against a live source before sending — adds a quality gate without the latency of manual review. Routing only flagged emails to humans provides targeted oversight where errors are detected, maintaining throughput for correct emails.

**A** is incorrect because pricing errors in customer communications create customer trust issues, potential contractual obligations, and financial risk; they cannot be accepted as normal failure.

**C** is incorrect because instructing the model not to make mistakes does not prevent hallucinated prices from entering the draft; structural validation is needed.

**D** is incorrect because scheduling does not prevent errors and does not reduce the number of incorrect emails sent.

[OpenAI Agents Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/agents)

questions.stem:
A business process manager wants to chain multiple AI tasks: extract action items from meeting notes, assign each to a team member, and draft a Slack message notifying each person of their task.

What is the MOST efficient architecture for this workflow?

options.text: Run a single ChatGPT prompt that does all three tasks simultaneously

Create a sequential pipeline where the output of each step (extracted items, assigned items, drafted messages) is the input to the next, with a validation checkpoint between assignment and notification
Have team members manually review meeting notes and assign their own tasks
Use ChatGPT only for the drafting step and do extraction and assignment manually

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because sequential pipelines with intermediate validation catch errors before they propagate. A validation step between assignment and notification is particularly important — if assignments are wrong, sending incorrect Slack notifications to team members would create confusion that is difficult to retract.

**A** is incorrect because a single prompt for three interdependent tasks typically produces less accurate results than specialized prompts for each step, and offers no checkpoint for catching errors mid-flow.

**C** is incorrect because manual processing defeats the automation benefit the manager is seeking.

**D** is incorrect because partial automation loses efficiency gains; the entire workflow benefits from AI assistance with appropriate checkpoints.

[OpenAI Agents Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/agents)

questions.stem:
A development team is using the OpenAI Responses API to build a coding assistant that maintains conversation history for a user session. As sessions grow long, the team notices increasing latency and cost.

What is the MOST effective strategy for managing this?

options.text: Limit sessions to 10 messages and force users to start over
Implement a context management strategy that summarizes earlier turns and retains only the most recent exchanges and relevant code snippets in the active context
Switch to a model with a smaller context window to reduce processing time
Send only the most recent user message without any conversation history

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because summarizing earlier turns compresses historical context while retaining semantic continuity, and retaining recent exchanges plus relevant code preserves the specific technical context the assistant needs. This reduces token count — and therefore cost and latency — without degrading the user experience.

**A** is incorrect because forcing session restarts loses conversational context that is valuable for a coding assistant; users would need to re-explain their code context.

**C** is incorrect because a smaller context window constrains the amount of information that can be processed and would cause the application to break for long sessions, not improve performance.

**D** is incorrect because sending only the most recent message loses all conversational context, making the assistant unable to understand references to previous steps or code defined earlier.

[OpenAI Responses API](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/responses)

questions.stem:
A product manager is deploying a custom GPT for use by her company's employees. She wants employees to be able to ask follow-up questions within a session but does not want them to override the GPT's core instructions.

What correctly describes the relationship between operator and user controls in this scenario?

options.text: Users can override operator system prompt instructions if they ask politely
The operator sets system-level instructions that take precedence over user requests; users can adjust behavior within the limits the operator permits
The product manager must approve each employee conversation in real time
Operators and users have equal authority over GPT behavior

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because the operator-user hierarchy in ChatGPT deployments grants operators (like the product manager) control over system-level instructions that govern the GPT's scope, persona, and restrictions. Users can interact flexibly within that defined scope but cannot override core operator constraints.

**A** is incorrect because user messages that attempt to override operator instructions should be resisted by the GPT; politeness does not constitute an authorization mechanism.

**C** is incorrect because operator control is implemented through system prompts and configuration, not through real-time conversation approval.

**D** is incorrect because operators and users explicitly have different authority levels; operator configuration takes precedence.

[OpenAI Agents Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/agents)

questions.stem: A team is designing quality control checkpoints for an AI agent that autonomously processes and responds to customer refund requests. Which TWO checkpoints would MOST effectively reduce risk in this pipeline? (Select TWO)

options.text: An automated rule check that verifies the refund amount is within policy limits before the agent triggers a transaction
A random daily log review by a team member to catch systematic errors the automated checks miss
Trusting the agent entirely since AI approval accuracy exceeds human accuracy in high-volume environments
Automatically escalating refund requests that trigger edge-case flags (e.g., unusually high amounts, repeated requests from the same account) to human review

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because a programmatic policy check before any financial transaction executes is a structural safeguard that catches out-of-policy refunds before they are issued — a critical checkpoint in any financial workflow.

**D** is correct because edge cases (high amounts, repeated requests, anomalous patterns) are where agent errors are most likely and most costly. Escalating these to humans provides targeted oversight without slowing the majority of straightforward cases.

**B** is partially useful but is incorrect as a primary checkpoint because random sampling catches only a fraction of errors and operates after the fact — errors have already occurred before the log review happens.

**C** is incorrect because automation accuracy claims do not eliminate the need for oversight, particularly in financial workflows where individual errors can be significant and systematic biases can cause widespread harm.

[OpenAI Agents Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/agents)

questions.stem:
A startup founder wants to use an AI agent to research competitors, draft market positioning statements, and update the company's pitch deck — all in a single automated run overnight.

What is the MOST important limitation to communicate to the founder before deployment?

options.text: The agent can only run during business hours

Multi-step agents that chain research, drafting, and document editing without human review can propagate early-step errors through all subsequent steps, potentially producing a polished but inaccurate pitch deck
Overnight runs are prohibited by OpenAI's terms of service
The agent cannot work with pitch deck files

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because error propagation is the core risk of fully autonomous multi-step agents. If the research step produces an incorrect competitor fact, the positioning statement will be built on that error, and the pitch deck will present it attractively — making the incorrect information more credible and harder to catch.

**A** is incorrect because AI agents can run at any time; there is no business-hours restriction.

**C** is incorrect because OpenAI's terms do not prohibit automated overnight API usage; programmatic use is the intended API use case.

**D** is incorrect because agents with appropriate tool integrations can work with documents; this is a capability question, not a fundamental limitation.

[OpenAI Agents Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/agents)

questions.stem:
A team is deciding between using direct ChatGPT prompts versus deploying an AI agent for a task. The task involves checking a single contract for three specific clause types and reporting back.

Which approach is MOST appropriate?

options.text: An autonomous agent with multiple tools and a multi-step plan
A direct ChatGPT prompt with the contract text and specific extraction instructions
A fully automated pipeline with human review disabled
An agent network with specialized subagents for each clause type

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because a well-defined, single-document, single-request task is ideally suited to direct prompting. Adding agent complexity — with tool invocation, planning loops, and multi-step execution — adds latency, cost, and failure modes without benefit for a task this straightforward.

**A** is incorrect because the task does not require multiple tools, external API calls, or iterative planning; agent overhead would be unjustified.

**C** is incorrect because removing human review from contract review is a responsible AI concern regardless of the task simplicity.

**D** is incorrect because a subagent network is architecturally designed for parallel complex tasks; three clause types in a single contract do not require distributed agent processing.

[OpenAI Agents Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/agents)
